event: Austronesian-speaking Lapita peoples settle the islands of Uvea (Wallis) and Futuna, leaving distinctive dentate-stamped pottery.
Lapita communities establish fishing and horticultural villages, cultivating taro, yams, and breadfruit across the islands.
Ancestral Polynesian society develops on Uvea and Futuna, with chiefly lineages and shared maritime culture linking the islands to Tonga and Samoa.
Tongan expansion brings Uvea under the influence of the Tu'i Tonga empire, reshaping its language and political hierarchy.
The kingdom of Uvea consolidates under a paramount chief, the Lavelua, with fortified earthworks and ring-ditch defenses still visible today.
Dutch navigators Jacob Le Maire and Willem Schouten reach Futuna and Alofi, naming them the Hoorn Islands after their home port.
British explorer Samuel Wallis sights the island of Uvea, which Europeans subsequently name Wallis Island after him.
French Marist missionaries led by Pierre Bataillon and Pierre Chanel arrive to evangelize Wallis and Futuna.
Missionary Pierre Chanel is killed on Futuna, becoming a martyr later canonized as the first saint of Oceania.
Following mass conversions to Catholicism, the Marist missionaries request French protection for the islands.
Queen Amelia of Uvea signs a treaty establishing a French protectorate over Wallis.
The kingdoms of Sigave and Alo on Futuna sign treaties placing the island under French protectorate as well.
Wallis and Futuna are administratively attached to the French colony of New Caledonia.
France formally extends colonial administration over the protectorate while preserving the traditional kingdoms.
United States forces establish a major military base on Wallis during the Pacific War, building an airfield and infrastructure.
American troops withdraw at the end of World War II, leaving behind roads and the wartime airstrip.
Inhabitants vote in a referendum in favor of becoming a French overseas territory.
Wallis and Futuna becomes a French overseas territory (territoire d'outre-mer) by the law of 29 July, retaining its three customary kingdoms.
The new statute takes effect, integrating the three monarchies of Uvea, Sigave, and Alo into the French Republic's administrative framework.
Wallis and Futuna gains representation with a deputy in the French National Assembly and a senator.
Tomasi Kulimoetoke remains the recognized Lavelua of Uvea, anchoring continuity of customary authority within the French territory.
Pope John Paul II visits Wallis and Futuna, underscoring the territory's deeply rooted Catholic identity.
A French constitutional reform reclassifies Wallis and Futuna as an overseas collectivity (collectivité d'outre-mer).
A succession crisis erupts on Wallis after King Tomasi Kulimoetoke shelters his grandson from French justice, sparking factional unrest.
King Tomasi Kulimoetoke II dies after a reign of nearly fifty years, deepening a prolonged dispute over the Uvea throne.
Kapiliele Faupala is enthroned as the new Lavelua of Uvea, though factional rivalries over the kingship persist.
King Kapiliele Faupala abdicates amid continued conflict between rival clans, leaving the Uvea throne vacant.
Patalione Kanimoa is installed as the new Lavelua of Uvea, partially resolving years of customary instability.
Population decline accelerates as out-migration to New Caledonia and metropolitan France draws away young residents.
Strict border controls and travel restrictions shield Wallis and Futuna from early COVID-19 outbreaks across the Pacific.
The territory records its first COVID-19 cases and launches vaccination campaigns despite its remote location.
Rising sea levels, coastal erosion, and stronger cyclones intensify climate-change threats to the low-lying islands and their freshwater supplies.
